meta.title: v-if vs v-show for Vue visibility toggles
meta.summary: A Vue screen hides and shows UI sections based on user interaction. Would you use v-if or v-show?

scenario: You are building a Vue 3 operations dashboard. The page has a filter drawer that users open and close many times while they work through a queue. The drawer contains search inputs, checkboxes, and a small amount of local UI state that users expect to still be there when they reopen it a few seconds later. One teammate wants to use `v-if` because hidden UI should not stay in the DOM. Another teammate wants to use `v-show` because the drawer is toggled frequently and should stay mounted while only changing visibility. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use v-if
options.summary: Fits best when the content is not shown often, is expensive to keep mounted, or should not exist until it is actually needed.
whenItWins: The section appears rarely, so paying mount and unmount cost only when needed is perfectly reasonable.
The hidden content is heavy enough that you do not want it sitting in the DOM all the time.
The UI should really disappear when hidden rather than stay mounted and only change visibility.
watchOutFor: If the element is toggled constantly, repeated mount and unmount can be more awkward than simply showing and hiding it.
A weak answer chooses v-if just because 'less DOM is always better' without checking how often the user toggles the UI.

options.label: Use v-show
options.summary: Fits best when the same UI is shown and hidden often and should stay mounted between quick toggles.
whenItWins: The user opens and closes the same panel frequently during normal workflow.
You want the UI to preserve its local state and avoid remounting on every toggle.
The content is not so heavy that keeping it mounted becomes the bigger cost.
watchOutFor: Keeping hidden content mounted can be wasteful if the section is rarely opened or much heavier than the current prompt suggests.
A weak answer says v-show is always faster without checking whether the content should really remain mounted.

decisionMatrix.title: Rarely opened or heavy content
decisionMatrix.prompt: The section appears infrequently or is expensive enough that keeping it mounted all the time is not attractive.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the content does not need to sit in the DOM between rare openings.
Still possible, but now you should explain why preserving mount state matters more than the cost of keeping the section around.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Users repeatedly open and close the same filter drawer and expect its local state to still be there during the workflow.

cells.note: Possible, but harder to defend because the repeated mount and unmount cycles fight the actual interaction pattern.
This is the cleanest answer because the panel is toggled often and benefits from staying mounted.

decisionMatrix.title: Hidden content should not stay mounted
decisionMatrix.prompt: The section should only exist when active, either for lifecycle reasons or because hidden presence itself is undesirable.

cells.note: This becomes easier to defend because conditional existence is part of the requirement, not just visibility.
At this point keeping the element mounted works against the shape of the requirement.

evaluationDimensions.title: How often does the UI toggle during normal use?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. Frequent toggles often push the answer toward keeping the same mounted subtree around.

evaluationDimensions.title: How expensive is mounting and unmounting the content?
evaluationDimensions.description: If the component is heavy or rare, paying that cost only when needed can be the right tradeoff.

evaluationDimensions.title: Should local UI state survive quick hide and show cycles?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should notice when the user experience expects the hidden panel to come back exactly as it was.

evaluationDimensions.title: Should the content still exist when hidden?
evaluationDimensions.description: Sometimes the real requirement is not just visual hiding. It is whether the subtree should remain mounted at all.

evaluationDimensions.title: Can you avoid oversimplified performance slogans?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should avoid turning this into 'one directive is always faster' and instead tie the choice to the actual interaction pattern.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean v-show because the prompt describes a frequently toggled panel that should stay mounted
strongAnswer.summary: This filter drawer is opened and closed repeatedly as part of normal work, and the user expects its local state to still be there. That makes `v-show` the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key clue is toggle frequency. This is not a rarely visited section. It is a panel the user keeps opening and closing during one workflow.
That makes `v-show` a better fit because the same mounted subtree can stay in place while only its visibility changes.
I would move toward `v-if` if the content were much heavier, opened rarely, or should truly not exist until the user activates it.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would use `v-show` for the filter drawer and reserve `v-if` for sections that are infrequent, heavier, or semantically absent when hidden.

interviewerPushback.question: Isn't v-if better because hidden DOM should not stay around?
interviewerPushback.answer: That can be true for rare or heavy content, but this prompt describes a panel that keeps toggling during the workflow. In that case keeping it mounted is part of the product experience, not waste by default.

interviewerPushback.question: When would v-if clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the content is rarely shown, expensive to keep mounted, or should only exist when active instead of merely becoming invisible.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the drawer became a rarely opened advanced panel or a much heavier subtree, I would move back toward v-if because preserving mount state would no longer be the biggest value.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use v-show because it is faster than v-if.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds like a slogan and skips the real questions of toggle frequency, mount cost, and state preservation.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use v-show because the drawer opens often and users may want the same state when they reopen it.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer boundary for when v-if becomes the stronger answer.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use `v-show` because the filter drawer is part of the user's active workflow and gets opened and closed repeatedly. I want the same mounted panel to stay available so its local state does not keep getting recreated on every toggle. I would use `v-if` instead for content that is rare, heavier, or should only exist when the user actually activates it.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from interaction pattern and state preservation instead of relying on a vague performance rule.

answerFramework: Start by asking how often the user will toggle the UI.
Check whether the hidden content should stay mounted or should disappear completely.
Choose the directive that matches that interaction pattern.
Explain the cost of the other option, either repeated remounting or unnecessary mounted content.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: v-show for frequent visibility toggles, v-if for rare or conditionally existing content.
antiPatterns: Saying v-if is always better because it removes DOM.
Saying v-show is always faster without discussing toggle frequency or mount cost.
Ignoring whether the user expects hidden UI state to still be there when they reopen it.
Treating visibility and conditional existence as if they were the same requirement.
